title: signalDrain double-decrements queueDepth: once for the drain signal and again for each resolved waiter, causing queueDepth to go below the true in-flight count
description: waitForDrain increments queueDepth before deciding whether to block (line 680), so each pending waiter already occupies one slot in the depth count. signalDrain correctly decrements once at line 692 for the chunk that the worker just consumed. Then inside the while-loop at line 701 it decrements again for every waiter it resolves, but those waiters correspond to pushes whose increments are still counted in queueDepth (their chunks have not been sent yet). This makes queueDepth go negative (floored at 0), causing the adaptive HWM to appear wider than configured: subsequent waitForDrain calls see a depth lower than reality and do not block when they should. Under sustained backpressure the worker queue grows beyond the configured HWM, defeating the backpressure contract.

title: pool.park then pool.unpark called back-to-back on the victim worker, immediately cancelling the parked state before assignWorker uses it
description: After the preemption ack resolves with 'paused', the scheduler records the victim's decoder as parked by calling pool.park(backgroundWorker) and sets workerPausedSession. On the very next line pool.unpark(backgroundWorker) removes the worker from the parked set (pool.ts line 276: parked.delete). The worker's activeSessionId is then manually set to RESERVED_SESSION_ID so assignWorker/bind can proceed. The pool.assertInvariants check in DEV mode will not fire here because park/unpark are called but pool.bind is not — however parkedCount will read 0 even though the logical 'paused session on worker' concept is live. The park/unpark pair achieves nothing and leaves the worker in an inconsistent state: it is in neither idle nor active nor parked, so pool.size accounts for it but no set tracks it between unpark and the subsequent pool.bind inside assignWorker.

title: On dedupe promotion the promoted record's state is mutated before the counter adjustment reads it, so the wrong counter gets incremented
description: When cancelling a primary with surviving subscribers, the code transfers worker/paused/queued state to promotedRecord (lines 541-560), which sets promotedRecord.state to 'running'/'paused'/'queued'. Then at line 572 it checks isSubscriber to do the promoted record's counter book-keeping. At line 575 it reads promotedRecord.state to decide which counter to increment — but if the transfer block set promotedRecord.state to 'paused' (line 550) or 'queued' (line 555), those new values are used. Subscriber records are created with state='running' (line 322 in acquireSlot), so a subscriber that was never reassigned still has state='running'. The paused transfer (line 550) correctly changes it, but if the worker transfer path (lines 541-547) was taken, promotedRecord.state stays 'running' which is correct. The queued transfer sets state='queued' (line 555) so _queuedCount gets incremented, but the subscriber was previously counted in _subscriberCount not _runningCount — so _runningCount is never decremented for the old subscriber entry yet _queuedCount is incremented, overcounting by 1.

title: workerId returned for a dedupe subscriber is -1 when primaryRecord has no worker yet (e.g. primary is still queued), but callers may treat -1 as a valid worker ID
description: When a dedupe subscriber joins and the primary session is still in the 'queued' state (no worker assigned yet), acquireSlot returns { workerId: -1 }. The expression is primaryRecord?.worker?.id ?? -1. The calling code in jxl-session presumably treats the workerId as informational, but if any caller branches on workerId !== -1 to decide whether the decode has started, it will incorrectly believe the decode has not started even after the primary gets a worker. There is no follow-up notification to subscribers when the primary transitions from queued to running, so the -1 is silently stale.

title: waitForDrain blocks when queueDepth >= hwm but the condition is < hwm (return early), meaning the blocking threshold is hwm not hwm-1, so HWM is effectively 1 less than configured
description: waitForDrain increments queueDepth then returns immediately if queueDepth < hwm, queuing a waiter otherwise. This means the Nth push (where N == hwm) blocks rather than being allowed through. For example with pushHwm=4 and adaptiveHwm()=4, the 4th push will see queueDepth==4 which is NOT < 4, so it blocks. Effectively only hwm-1 pushes are in-flight at a time. The comment says 'Backpressure high-water mark: pushes after this depth block' which implies pushes AT this depth should also be allowed. Whether this is a spec bug or implementation bug depends on intent, but the mismatch between the name 'high-water mark' (typically the maximum allowed before blocking) and the < comparison (blocks at == hwm) means actual capacity is pushHwm-1.

title: In tryPreempt's 'terminal' branch the victim session is already cleaned up by handleWorkerMessage, but tryPreempt does not decrement _runningCount or update the victim record before acquiring the new worker
description: When the victim emits a terminal message (decode_final etc.) during the preemption window, handleWorkerMessage fires first: it calls cleanupSession(victimSessionId) and releases the worker to the pool. The ack handler then resolves with resolvedKind='terminal'. In the terminal branch (line 905), tryPreempt calls pool.acquire() and then assignWorker. By this point cleanupSession has already run for the victim, so the session is fully cleaned up. However preemptionCount is incremented (line 914), which is fine. The concern is that if pool.acquire() returns null here (pool is contested), the function returns null — but the visible session that caused the preemption attempt is not re-queued. The acquireSlot caller then receives null from tryPreempt and falls through to the queue path, which is correct. This is not a bug by itself but the control flow is subtle and lacks a comment explaining why no re-queue is needed here.

title: adjustSessionCount guards against negative values with Math.max(0,...) but silently masks counter drift rather than asserting in DEV mode
description: The counters _runningCount, _queuedCount, _pausedCount, and _subscriberCount are all clamped to 0 via Math.max rather than asserting. If a double-decrement or missed increment occurs (like the promotion queued-transfer bug in logic-3), the counters silently stabilize at 0 instead of going negative, making getMetrics() return plausible-looking values. Adding a DEV-mode assertion (like pool.ts's assertInvariants pattern) when a counter would go below 0 would catch counter drift bugs at the point of mutation rather than letting them propagate silently to monitoring dashboards.

category: no property-based testing on math/state-heavy code
title: CoreBudget drainWaiters and the backpressure signalDrain loop share the same ring-buffer compaction pattern but have no shared invariant tests verifying tokens never go negative or exceed capacity after arbitrary acquire/release sequences
description: Both CoreBudget.drainWaiters (budget.ts) and Scheduler.signalDrain (scheduler.ts) implement the same amortised copyWithin compaction pattern. Neither has a property-based test driving arbitrary acquire/release sequences to verify that available tokens stay within [0, capacity] and that waiter FIFO order is preserved. The existing tests use deterministic fixed sequences. A fast-check or similar property test would have caught the signalDrain double-decrement (logic-1) since it would produce negative effective depths under random burst patterns.
